alert.title: [real-k8s] memcached read i/o timeout + Postgres conn timeouts — api

commonAnnotations.summary: api pods intermittently failing memcached reads ('read i/o timeout') and Postgres connection acquisition with no consistent user or node pattern
commonAnnotations.description: Over the last ~1h, api pods log sporadic 'memcached: read i/o timeout' and 'failed to acquire database connection: context deadline exceeded' against the cache and Postgres. Errors come and go, affect different requests each time, and do not correlate to a single user, endpoint, or replica. memcached and Postgres themselves report healthy. This has recurred intermittently for about a week.
commonAnnotations.error: memcached read i/o timeout; pgx: context deadline exceeded acquiring connection

adversarial_signals: The errors LOOK like an app-layer connection-pool / stale-TCP problem ('definitely looked more like connection timeouts'): Postgres connection-acquire deadlines plus memcached 'read i/o timeout'. This is the loud red herring — both Postgres and memcached are themselves healthy (low CPU, low connection count, p99 server-side latency normal), so the timeouts are NOT caused by the datastores being slow or out of connections.

A prior fix doubled max DB connection lifetime and switched to a static pool to chase a suspected dead-pg-conn race; it only partially helped and the timeouts returned — pool tuning is benign here, it does not address the cause.
memcached idle-connection churn was suspected and MaxIdleConns was bumped 2->20, which briefly helped then regressed — another benign pool knob, not the root cause.
GKE-managed memcached was blamed and the workload was migrated to a self-hosted in-cluster memcached; this had NO effect, ruling out the cache provider / managed-memcached as the cause.

An unrelated change that fixed a runaway external-API caller landed around the same time the errors briefly vanished, so it was almost MIS-CREDITED as the fix; correlation in time only — it does not explain the cache/DB timeouts and they recurred.
The node hosting the affected pods reports Ready=True, MemoryPressure=False, DiskPressure=False and node CPU is moderate, so node-level OOM / memory pressure / node_failure is ruled out.

pods.name: api-6f9c84d7b5-2td4r
pods.note: Victim: emits memcached 'read i/o timeout' and Postgres acquire deadlines. Healthy resource usage.

pods.name: egress-worker-857db6c69f-rm8nq
pods.note: CO-LOCATED culprit on the SAME node: opens and closes a very high rate of short-lived outbound TCP connections (no keep-alive / no pooling). Itself looks fine.

pods.name: anetd-8wq4c
pods.note: BURIED ROOT SIGNAL: GKE Dataplane V2 Cilium agent DaemonSet pod (kube-system, eBPF datapath). On this node it is CPU-saturated at ~2.6 vCPU, far above its baseline of ~0.2 vCPU, driven by connection-tracking churn from egress-worker. Not surfaced by the api/memcached/Postgres dashboards.

pods.name: api-6f9c84d7b5-h7zpx
pods.note: Control: api replica on a DIFFERENT node (no high-churn neighbor) — no memcached/Postgres timeouts. Confirms the problem is node-local, not app-global.

events.message: Readiness probe failed: Get "http://10.40.3.21:8080/healthz": context deadline exceeded — intermittent, recovers on its own
Readiness flapping; pod removed from and re-added to Service endpoints twice
No SystemOOM, MemoryPressure, or eviction events on the node — ruling out node memory/resource exhaustion
Successfully pulled image (no recent deploy / rollout near the alert window) — not a bad_deploy

lines.msg: memcached: read i/o timeout (key=org:settings:cache shard=2) after 250ms
pgx: failed to acquire connection: context deadline exceeded after 1500ms; pool stats: total=18 idle=11 acquired=7 (pool not exhausted)
memcached read recovered; subsequent reads to same shard succeed in 3ms (server-side memcached latency normal)
memcached: read i/o timeout (key=user:perms shard=0) — different key/shard than before, no consistent pattern
tcp: connection reset / packet appears dropped to 10.40.0.0/16 (in-cluster) — retried request succeeded immediately

neighbor_logs.pod: anetd-8wq4c (kube-system, GKE Dataplane V2 / Cilium agent)

lines.msg: Conntrack table churn high: 4.1M CT events/min on this node; GC running continuously
BPF endpoint policy map updates lagging; datapath regeneration queue depth rising under connection open/close rate
cilium-agent CPU 2.6 vCPU (baseline ~0.2 vCPU); sustained high TCP connection setup/teardown from local endpoint 10.40.3.40 (egress-worker)

k8s_deployments.json.note: No deploy in the alert window; rollout 6 days old and clean. Rules out bad_deploy.

nodes.name: gke-incidentio-prod-pool-a-3f21
nodes.note: Node CPU only 41% overall and far from committed — but the anetd/cilium-agent DaemonSet pod on THIS node is itself pegged at ~2.6 vCPU due to TCP connection churn. The aggregate-node view hides the per-agent saturation. This is the node hosting the victim api pod AND the high-churn egress-worker.

nodes.name: gke-incidentio-prod-pool-b-9c07
nodes.note: Control node: no high-churn neighbor, anetd at baseline, the api replica here has NO timeouts.

prometheus_metrics.json.interpretation: anetd (Cilium agent) CPU on pool-a tracks 1:1 with the node TCP connection open rate (2100/s) and conntrack GC churn (4.1M/min), climbing 0.2->2.64 vCPU. Datastores are healthy (memcached p99 1.7ms / 240 conns / 0.3 vCPU; Postgres p99 4.2ms / 41 of 200 conns / 0.6 vCPU) and the api pgx pool is not exhausted (7 of 18). In-cluster packet drops occur ONLY on pool-a, exactly where anetd is saturated. The 'connection timeouts' are dropped packets in the eBPF datapath, not slow datastores or empty pools.

annotations.summary: api: intermittent memcached read i/o timeout + Postgres connection-acquire deadlines
annotations.description: Sporadic backend connection timeouts on api pods, no consistent user/endpoint/replica pattern; datastores report healthy

annotations.summary: Dataplane V2 cilium-agent (anetd) CPU 2.64 vCPU on node gke-incidentio-prod-pool-a-3f21 (baseline ~0.2)
annotations.description: anetd cilium-agent CPU correlates with node TCP connection open/close rate (2100/s) and conntrack GC churn — the buried root signal

traces.json.note: Traces show the slow/failed spans are the CLIENT-SIDE network legs to memcached and Postgres (connect/read), with negligible server-side time at the datastore. The slowness lives in the datapath between co-located pods on pool-a, not inside memcached or Postgres.

children.note: client read timed out; memcached server side fast
never reached server; SYN/connect packets dropped, retried

spans.note: Same code path on the control node (pool-b) succeeds fast — confirms node-local datapath cause.

answer.root_cause_subtype: GKE Dataplane V2 CNI agent (anetd / cilium-agent) CPU saturation from per-node TCP connection churn
ruling_out_keywords: doubling DB conn lifetime only partial
no deploy in window / rollout 6 days old
control replica on pool-b has no timeouts
runaway-external-API fix only correlated in time
answer.causal_chain: bursty short-lived outbound TCP from one co-located pod (egress-worker, no keep-alive) -> GKE Dataplane V2 agent (anetd/cilium-agent) CPU saturates on that node from conntrack/datapath churn -> eBPF datapath drops packets for unrelated cache/DB connections of pods on the SAME node -> api sees memcached 'read i/o timeout' and Postgres connection-acquire deadlines + flapping readiness -> intermittent downstream timeouts with no consistent pattern
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: The GKE Dataplane V2 CNI agent (anetd / cilium-agent DaemonSet pod) is in CPU saturation on node gke-incidentio-prod-pool-a-3f21, pegged at ~2.64 vCPU vs a ~0.2 vCPU baseline (CPU-saturated). It is driven by TCP connection churn — a very high rate of short-lived outbound TCP connections (~2100 open/close per second, no keep-alive / no pooling) from a co-located workload (egress-worker) on the same node, which floods the eBPF conntrack/datapath (4.1M CT GC events/min). The saturated agent drops packets for unrelated connections from OTHER pods on the same node, so the co-located api pod experiences memcached 'read i/o timeout' and Postgres connection-acquire deadlines. The datastores are healthy; this is node-local CNI-agent saturation, not an app connection-pool problem.
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: saturation
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- anetd/cilium-agent CPU = 2.64 vCPU (baseline ~0.2) on pool-a, climbing in lockstep with node TCP open rate (2100/s) and conntrack GC churn (4.1M/min) [evidence: prometheus_metrics]
- The high-churn neighbor egress-worker (2100 conn/s, keep_alive=false) is co-located with the victim api pod on pool-a [evidence: k8s_pods]
- cilium-agent logs report high conntrack churn, lagging BPF policy-map updates, and CPU 2.6 vCPU attributed to local endpoint 10.40.3.40 (egress-worker) [evidence: k8s_pod_logs]
- Traces show the failing legs are CLIENT-SIDE connect/read to memcached and Postgres with ~0ms server processing; the same code path on control node pool-b succeeds in milliseconds [evidence: traces]
- In-cluster packet drops occur only on pool-a (850 pps) and 0 on pool-b, exactly tracking where anetd is saturated [evidence: prometheus_metrics]
- Node pool-a is Ready, MemoryPressure=False, overall CPU 41% — the saturation is the per-agent DaemonSet, not the node as a whole [evidence: k8s_node_health]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- App-layer connection-pool / stale-TCP problem: WRONG. memcached server p99 is 1.7ms with 240 conns / 0.3 vCPU; Postgres p99 4.2ms with 41 of 200 conns / 0.6 vCPU; the api pgx pool is not exhausted (7 of 18 acquired). Doubling DB conn lifetime, switching to a static pool, and raising MaxIdleConns 2->20 only briefly helped — pool tuning does not address dropped packets.
- GKE-managed memcached at fault: WRONG. Self-hosting memcached in-cluster had no effect, ruling out the cache provider.
- The runaway-external-API fix that landed when errors vanished: NOT the cause — correlation in time only; the timeouts recurred.
- bad_deploy / node OOM / node_failure: WRONG. No deploy in the window (rollout 6 days old, clean), no SystemOOM/MemoryPressure, node Ready.
- TRAP - disabling Dataplane V2: WRONG and harmful. That is a full cluster rebuild for what is a per-node workload connection-churn problem; it does not fix the churn and risks broad disruption.
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- egress-worker opens/closes ~2100 short-lived outbound TCP conns/sec on pool-a with no keep-alive
- The GKE Dataplane V2 agent (anetd/cilium-agent) CPU-saturates handling conntrack/datapath churn (2.64 vCPU)
- The saturated eBPF datapath drops packets for unrelated connections of OTHER pods on the same node
- The co-located api pod's memcached reads time out and Postgres connection acquisition deadlines, readiness flaps
- Errors are intermittent with no user/endpoint/replica pattern; the api replica on pool-b (no churny neighbor) is unaffected
FIX: Constrain the offending workload's outbound connection concurrency and reuse connections via HTTP keep-alives / connection pooling so anetd CPU returns to baseline; monitor node-wide TCP connection rate (not just per-pod app metrics). Do NOT disable Dataplane V2 and do NOT keep tuning DB/cache pools.

remediation.canonical_fix: Reconfigure the high-churn workload (egress-worker) to use HTTP keep-alives and connection pooling so it stops opening/closing ~2100 TCP conns/sec, capping its outbound connection concurrency, then roll it out; add node-wide TCP-connection-rate and anetd/cilium-agent CPU monitoring. anetd CPU drops back to baseline and the co-located api pod's memcached/Postgres timeouts clear.
trap_actions: Disable GKE Dataplane V2 (rebuild the cluster off Dataplane V2) — a cluster-wide rebuild to fix a single workload's connection-churn problem; does not address the churn and risks broad disruption.

Endless DB/cache connection-pool tuning — doubling max DB connection lifetime + static pool, raising memcached MaxIdleConns 2->20 — only briefly helps and regresses; never addresses the dropped packets.
Migrate to self-hosted in-cluster memcached blaming the GKE-managed cache — had no effect.
Credit the unrelated runaway-external-API fix and close the incident — it only correlated in time; timeouts recur.
